title: CPU Architecture and the Fetch-Decode-Execute Cycle
key_insight: The CPU's entire existence is one loop: fetch instruction → decode it → execute it. Billions of times per second.
body:
## The Fetch-Decode-Execute Cycle

Every CPU follows this loop constantly:

```
1. FETCH    → Read next instruction from memory
2. DECODE   → Figure out what the instruction means
3. EXECUTE  → Do it (add numbers, move data, compare values, jump)
4. → Repeat
```

Modern CPUs execute 3–5 billion cycles per second (3–5 GHz). Each cycle can process multiple instructions through pipelining.

## CPU vs GPU: Architecture Comparison

```
CPU (optimised for latency):
┌──────┬──────┬──────┬──────┐
│ Core │ Core │ Core │ Core │  ← 4 powerful cores
│  ALU │  ALU │  ALU │  ALU │
│Cache │Cache │Cache │Cache │
└──────┴──────┴──────┴──────┘
  Control logic: complex (branch prediction, out-of-order execution)

GPU (optimised for throughput):
┌─┬─┬─┬─┬─┬─┬─┬─┬─┬─┬─┬─┐
│c│c│c│c│c│c│c│c│c│c│c│c│
│c│c│c│c│c│c│c│c│c│c│c│c│  ← Thousands of simple cores
│c│c│c│c│c│c│c│c│c│c│c│c│
└─┴─┴─┴─┴─┴─┴─┴─┴─┴─┴─┴─┘
  Control logic: simple (all cores do the same thing)
```

## SIMD: CPU Parallelism

Modern CPUs have SIMD (Single Instruction, Multiple Data) extensions — they can apply one operation to 4–16 values at once:

```python
import numpy as np
# NumPy uses SIMD automatically:
a = np.array([1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8])
b = np.array([8, 7, 6, 5, 4, 3, 2, 1])
c = a + b  # All 8 additions happen simultaneously via SIMD
```

This is why NumPy is 100× faster than a Python loop — it uses SIMD instructions.

## When to Use CPU vs GPU

| Task | CPU | GPU |
|------|-----|-----|
| Data preprocessing | ✓ | |
| Training | | ✓ |
| Inference (batch) | | ✓ |
| Inference (single request) | ✓ | ✓ |
| Anything with Python control flow | ✓ | |
common_mistakes: Running preprocessing on GPU — CPU is better for data augmentation, tokenisation, and other sequential operations
Bottlenecking on Python loops instead of NumPy — any loop that can be vectorised in NumPy should be
Not pinning tensors to CPU memory before GPU transfer — unpinned memory transfers are slower
mini_exercise: Compare loop vs NumPy: time `sum([i**2 for i in range(1_000_000)])` vs `import numpy as np; np.sum(np.arange(1_000_000)**2)`. Measure with time.perf_counter(). NumPy uses SIMD.
